subject: ap-precalculus
title: Rational Functions: Covariation, Asymptotic Behavior & Model Validity
stimulus: A car dealership models the resale value of a used vehicle using the function $V$ given by $V(x) = \frac{28}{1 + 0.4x}$ for $x \geq 0$, where $V(x)$ is the value in thousands of dollars and $x$ is the number of years since purchase. The function $M$ gives the cumulative maintenance cost of the car, in thousands of dollars, as a function of $x$, and is given by $M(x) = 0.15 \cdot (1.2)^x$.

parts.prompt:
(i) Find the average rate of change of $V$ from $x = 0$ to $x = 2$, and from $x = 2$ to $x = 4$. Express answers in thousands of dollars per year. Show supporting work.

(ii) Based on the two average rates of change found in part (i), determine whether the average rate of change of $V$ is increasing or decreasing as $x$ increases from $0$ to $4$. Describe what this tells you about the concavity of the graph of $V$ on this interval, and interpret what this means in the context of car depreciation.
parts.scoring_notes: Exact values: $V(0) = 28$, $V(2) = 28/1.8 \approx 15.556$, $V(4) = 28/2.6 \approx 10.769$. Rate on $[0,2]$: $(15.556 - 28)/2 \approx -6.222$ thousand dollars/year. Rate on $[2,4]$: $(10.769 - 15.556)/2 \approx -2.394$ thousand dollars/year. For part (ii), the rate goes from $-6.222$ to $-2.394$, which is increasing (becoming less negative). An increasing rate of change means concave up. In context, the rate of depreciation is slowing — the car loses value more gradually over time. All three elements required for the second point: (1) rate is increasing, (2) concave up, (3) contextual meaning.

scoring_points.description: Both correct average rates of change: approximately $-6.222$ thousand dollars/year on $[0,2]$ and approximately $-2.394$ thousand dollars/year on $[2,4]$; supporting work showing $V(0)$, $V(2)$, $V(4)$ and the difference quotients
wrong_examples: Rate on $[0,2] \approx -12.444$ — forgot to divide by the interval length of $2$
$V(2) = 28/(0.4 \cdot 2) = 35$ — incorrect denominator; the denominator is $1 + 0.4(2) = 1.8$, not $0.4 \cdot 2$
common_traps: The denominator of $V(x)$ is $1 + 0.4x$, not $0.4x$ — substitute carefully: $V(2) = 28/(1 + 0.8) = 28/1.8$
Both rates are negative (depreciation), but they are being compared by magnitude — $-2.39 > -6.22$ on the number line
scoring_points.official_rubric: The point is earned for both correct average rates of change — approximately $-6.222$ thousand dollars/year on $[0,2]$ and $-2.394$ thousand dollars/year on $[2,4]$ — with supporting work showing $V(0)$, $V(2)$, $V(4)$, and both difference quotients.

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly evaluates $V$ at all three points, computes both difference quotients with the correct denominator $2$, and reports negative rates reflecting depreciation.
Computes the difference in values but forgets to divide by the interval length of $2$. The average rate of change requires division by $\Delta x = 2$.
Uses incorrect denominator $0.4x$ instead of $1 + 0.4x$. The correct substitution is $V(2) = 28/(1 + 0.8) = 28/1.8 \approx 15.556$.

scoring_points.description: Correct conclusion with all three elements: (1) average rate of change is increasing (becoming less negative), (2) graph is concave up, (3) contextual interpretation that depreciation is slowing

required_elements: rate of change is increasing (from $\approx -6.222$ to $\approx -2.394$, becoming less negative)
graph of $V$ is concave up on $[0,4]$
in context: the car is still losing value but depreciating more slowly as it ages
alternatives.correct_example: The average rate of change increases from $-6.222$ to $-2.394$ (becomes less negative), so $V$ is concave up. In context, the car continues to lose value but the rate of depreciation slows as the car ages — it loses value most rapidly when new.

wrong_examples: 'Decreasing rate of change' — incorrect; $-2.394 > -6.222$, so the rate is increasing (less negative)
'Concave down' — incorrect; increasing rate of change corresponds to concave up, not concave down
common_traps: When rates of change are negative, an 'increasing' rate means becoming less negative (closer to zero), which corresponds to concave UP — not concave down
All three elements (direction of rate, concavity, context) are required; missing any one element does not earn the point
scoring_points.official_rubric: The point is earned for all three required elements: (1) the average rate of change is increasing (from $\approx -6.222$ to $\approx -2.394$, becoming less negative), (2) the graph is concave up, and (3) a contextual interpretation that the rate of depreciation is slowing. Missing any one element does not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: The rate changes from $-6.222$ to $-2.394$, which is increasing (less negative). An increasing rate of change means the graph of $V$ is concave up. In context, the car is still depreciating, but it loses value more slowly as it ages.
sample_responses.commentary: Addresses all three required elements: increasing rate, concave up interpretation, and a contextual statement about slowing depreciation.

sample_responses.response: The rate of change goes from $-6.222$ to $-2.394$, which is decreasing because the values are getting larger in magnitude.
sample_responses.commentary: Confuses the direction — on the number line $-2.394 > -6.222$, so the rate is increasing (becoming less negative). A common error is comparing absolute magnitudes instead of signed values.

sample_responses.response: Rate is increasing; graph is concave up.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies the increasing rate and concave up behavior but omits the contextual interpretation about depreciation slowing. All three elements are required.

parts.prompt:
(i) Determine the end behavior of $V(x)$ as $x$ increases without bound. Express your answer using the notation of a limit, and interpret it in the context of car value.

(ii) Find the value of $x$ at which the cumulative maintenance cost $M(x)$ equals the car's resale value $V(x)$. Express your answer as a decimal approximation. Show or describe the method used, and explain what this value of $x$ represents for a car owner.
parts.scoring_notes: For part (i), as $x \to \infty$, $1 + 0.4x \to \infty$, so $V(x) = 28/(1+0.4x) \to 0$. The limit is $\lim_{x \to \infty} V(x) = 0$. All four limit notation components are required: 'lim', '$x \to \infty$', the function name $V$ (or the full expression), and the value $0$. The contextual note that the value approaches but never reaches zero earns the interpretation component. For part (ii), the equation $28/(1+0.4x) = 0.15(1.2)^x$ is transcendental and requires technology. A graphing calculator or table intersection near $x \approx 8.0$ is expected (accept $[7.8, 8.2]$). The interpretation must connect the crossover to a practical ownership decision.

scoring_points.description: $\lim_{x \to \infty} V(x) = 0$ with all four notation components present AND contextual interpretation that the car's value approaches but never reaches zero

required_elements: contextual interpretation: the car's resale value approaches $\$0$ thousand but never actually equals zero
alternatives.correct_example: $\lim_{x \to \infty} V(x) = 0$. As years increase without bound, the car's resale value approaches $\$0$ but never reaches zero — the horizontal asymptote is $y = 0$.

wrong_examples: Writing 'V approaches 0 as x gets large' without limit notation — correct idea but does not earn the point; all four notation components are required
$\lim_{x \to \infty} V(x) = 28$ — incorrect; $28$ is the initial value ($x=0$), not the limit at infinity
common_traps: The limit notation must include all four components: 'lim', '$x \to \infty$', the function name, and the value — omitting any one component is an error
The model has a horizontal asymptote at $y = 0$, not a vertical asymptote — $V$ is always positive and approaches zero from above
scoring_points.official_rubric: The point is earned for a correct limit statement $\lim_{x \to \infty} V(x) = 0$ with all four notation components ('lim', '$x \to \infty$', $V$, $0$) AND a contextual interpretation. Omitting the 'lim' notation or stating the wrong limit value does not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: $\lim_{x \to \infty} V(x) = 0$. As $x$ increases without bound, the car's resale value approaches $\$0$ thousand but never reaches zero — the value is always positive.
sample_responses.commentary: Contains all four required limit notation components and provides a contextual interpretation about the car's value asymptotically approaching zero.
Correctly identifies the limiting behavior but does not use the required 'lim' notation. All four components of limit notation must be present.
States the initial value ($V(0) = 28$) as the limit instead of the horizontal asymptote value. As $x \to \infty$, the denominator $1 + 0.4x \to \infty$, so $V(x) \to 0$.

scoring_points.description: Correct crossover value $x \approx 8.0$ years (accept $[7.8, 8.2]$) from technology, with equation setup shown AND contextual interpretation of the crossover point

required_elements: interpretation: at ~8 years the maintenance cost equals the car's value, suggesting it may be time to sell
alternatives.correct_example: Set $V(x) = M(x)$: $\frac{28}{1+0.4x} = 0.15(1.2)^x$. Using a graphing calculator, intersection near $x \approx 8.0$. At $\approx 8$ years, the cumulative maintenance cost equals the car's resale value — beyond this point maintenance exceeds car value.

wrong_examples: Solving algebraically — this equation has no closed-form solution; technology is required and must be mentioned
$x \approx 0.15$ — this is the initial maintenance cost, not the crossover point
common_traps: $V$ and $M$ are both in thousands of dollars — no unit conversion needed before setting them equal
The crossover point is a practical decision point: beyond $x \approx 8$ years, maintenance costs exceed resale value
scoring_points.official_rubric: The point is earned for the crossover value $x \approx 8.0$ years (accept $[7.8, 8.2]$) obtained by technology with the equation $V(x) = M(x)$ set up AND a contextual interpretation of the crossover point for a car owner.

sample_responses.response: Set $\frac{28}{1+0.4x} = 0.15(1.2)^x$. Using a graphing calculator to find the intersection, $x \approx 8.0$ years. At this point, the cumulative maintenance cost equals the car's resale value — beyond $\approx 8$ years, maintenance costs exceed what the car is worth.
sample_responses.commentary: Sets up the correct equation, uses technology to find the intersection, and interprets the crossover as the point where maintenance costs match resale value.
Finds the correct crossover value but provides no contextual interpretation. The meaning of this crossover for a car owner must be explained.

parts.prompt:
A financial analyst proposes an alternative model for the car's total cost of ownership, $T(x) = V(x) + M(x)$, defined as the sum of the car's current resale value and cumulative maintenance cost.

(i) Find the value of $T(3)$ as a decimal approximation. Show the computations that lead to your answer.

(ii) Using technology, determine the approximate value of $x$ at which $T(x)$ achieves its minimum on $0 \leq x \leq 15$. Interpret the meaning of this minimum value in the context of car ownership.
parts.scoring_notes: For part (i), $V(3) = 28/(1+1.2) = 28/2.2 \approx 12.727$ thousand dollars; $M(3) = 0.15 \times (1.2)^3 = 0.15 \times 1.728 = 0.259$ thousand dollars; $T(3) \approx 12.986$ thousand dollars (accept $[12.97, 13.00]$). Both components must be shown separately before adding. For part (ii), using technology, $T(x)$ decreases initially (as the steep drop in $V$ outpaces the slow growth of $M$) then increases once $M$ grows exponentially and $V$ has flattened. The minimum occurs near $x \approx 8.0$ to $8.5$ years (accept $[7.5, 9.0]$). Interpretation must explain what the minimum represents in the car ownership context.

scoring_points.description: $T(3) \approx 12.986$ thousand dollars (accept $[12.97, 13.00]$); supporting work must show $V(3)$ and $M(3)$ calculated separately before summing
common_traps: $(1.2)^3 = 1.728$, not $3.6$ — evaluate the exponent before multiplying by $0.15$
Supporting work must show both $V(3)$ and $M(3)$ individually; jumping straight to a sum without showing components does not earn the point
scoring_points.official_rubric: The point is earned for $T(3) \approx 12.986$ thousand dollars (accept $[12.97, 13.00]$) with supporting work showing $V(3)$ and $M(3)$ computed separately. Jumping to a sum without showing both components does not earn the point.

sample_responses.commentary: Shows both components ($V(3)$ and $M(3)$) calculated individually with correct intermediate values, then adds them to obtain $T(3)$.
Adds $0.15$ (the initial maintenance coefficient) instead of $0.15 \cdot (1.2)^3 = 0.259$. The exponent must be evaluated: $M(3) = 0.15 \cdot 1.728 \approx 0.259$.

scoring_points.description: Correct minimum near $x \approx 8.0$–$8.5$ years (accept $[7.5, 9.0]$) from technology AND interpretation that the minimum represents the year at which combined cost (value + maintenance) is lowest

required_elements: minimum of $T(x)$ near $x \approx 8.0$ to $8.5$ years using technology (accept $[7.5, 9.0]$)
interpretation: at ~8 years, the sum of resale value and maintenance is at its lowest point
alternatives.correct_example: Using a graphing calculator, $T(x) = \frac{28}{1+0.4x} + 0.15(1.2)^x$ has a minimum near $x \approx 8.3$ years on $[0,15]$. This represents the year at which the combined resale value and maintenance cost is minimized — the optimal point in the car's ownership lifecycle.

wrong_examples: $T$ has no minimum — incorrect; the sum of a decreasing rational and an increasing exponential must have a minimum where the rates balance
Minimum at $x = 0$ — this is the maximum of $T$, not the minimum; $T(0) = 28 + 0.15 \approx 28.15$
common_traps: $T$ decreases initially because $V$ drops steeply while $M$ grows slowly; eventually $M$ grows exponentially and dominates, pushing $T$ upward — the minimum is in between
An interpretation is required, not just the $x$-value; state what the minimum represents in context
scoring_points.official_rubric: The point is earned for the approximate minimum location near $x \approx 8.0$–$8.5$ years (accept $[7.5, 9.0]$) found by technology AND an interpretation that the minimum represents the year at which combined cost (resale value + maintenance) is lowest. Stating only the $x$-value without interpretation does not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Using a graphing calculator, $T(x)$ has a minimum near $x \approx 8.3$ years on $[0, 15]$. This is the year at which the sum of the car's resale value and cumulative maintenance cost is at its lowest — the optimal point in the car's ownership lifecycle.
sample_responses.commentary: Uses technology to locate the minimum, gives an approximate $x$-value within the accepted range, and provides a contextual interpretation.
Finds the correct approximate minimum location but provides no contextual interpretation. The meaning of this minimum in the car ownership context is required.
